title: Templates
narrativeIntro: Components need a face. The template is where data becomes visible — where values become text, where arrays become lists, where conditions become presence or absence. The Drift's visual language is its template syntax: binding, directives, pipes. Learn to speak it and the structures show you their contents.
narrativeOutro: Templates are the bridge between data and perception. What lives in your TypeScript now renders in the view, and what the user touches flows back. The loop is closing.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland develops better visual interfaces for his systems. You learn Angular template syntax.
conceptsPreview: @for, @if, property binding, event binding, and pipes

challenges.title: Control Flow Output
challenges.narrativeHook: Two control flow blocks determine what renders and how many times. Trace the output.
challenges.instructions:
Read the component template below. `@for` iterates the array — it creates one host element per item. `@if` conditionally renders content inside.

Without running it, count how many `<li>` elements render and how many `<span>` elements render.
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// 3 <li> elements (one per item in @for), but only 2 <span> elements (only where @if (item.active) is true).
challenges.predictPrompt: How many <li> elements render, and how many <span> elements render?

explainGate.prompt: Why does @for create a <li> for Beta even though Beta's <span> is not rendered?

hints: @for iterates every item in the array. It creates one host <li> per item regardless of anything inside.
@if on the inner <span> only controls whether the span renders — the parent <li> is independent and always created by @for.
3 items → 3 <li> elements. But only Alpha and Gamma have active: true → 2 <span> elements.

challenges.title: Signal List
challenges.narrativeHook: The sensor array is transmitting. Render only the active signals.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `SignalListComponent` template: - `@for` already iterates `signals` (tracking by `signal.id`) - Inside the `@for` block, add `@if` to only render active signals - Each active signal renders as `
  • {{ signal.id }}: {{ signal.strength }}
    • ` The class and `@for` scaffold are already in place — add the `@if` block.

files.name: signal-list.component.ts

tests.testCode: const c = new SignalListComponent(); if (c.signals.length !== 4) throw new Error(`signals should have 4 items, got ${c.signals.length}`);
const c = new SignalListComponent(); if (c.activeCount !== 3) throw new Error(`activeCount should be 3, got ${c.activeCount}`);
const c = new SignalListComponent(); const a2 = c.signals.find(s => s.id === 'A-2'); if (a2.active) throw new Error('A-2 should have active: false');

tests.description: Template must use @if to filter active signals
tests.testCode: if (!SIGNAL_TEMPLATE.includes('@if')) throw new Error('Template must use @if (signal.active) { ... } to filter active signals — add the @if block inside @for');

tests.description: Template should render an <li> element inside the @if block
tests.testCode: if (!SIGNAL_TEMPLATE.includes('<li>')) throw new Error('Template should contain an <li> element inside the @if block — add the list item');
if (!SIGNAL_TEMPLATE.includes('signal.strength')) throw new Error('Template should display signal.strength — check your interpolation inside <li>');

hints:
Put the
  • element inside the @if block: @if (signal.active) {
  • {{ signal.id }}: {{ signal.strength }}
    • }
Unlike *ngFor/*ngIf (which cannot both go on the same element), @for and @if are block-level — @if nests cleanly inside @for with no wrapper needed.

challenges.title: Binding Reversed
challenges.narrativeHook: The button should disable when the system is locked. It disables when it's not. The logic is inverted.
challenges.instructions:
This component has a `locked` property. The launch button should be **disabled when `locked` is `true`** — but currently it's disabled when `locked` is `false`.

There is exactly one bug. Fix the binding.

files.name: launch-control.component.ts

tests.testCode: const c = new LaunchControlComponent(); if (!c.locked) throw new Error('locked should start as true');

tests.description: launch() should set launched to true
tests.testCode: const c = new LaunchControlComponent(); c.launch(); if (!c.launched) throw new Error('launch() should set launched to true');
const c = new LaunchControlComponent(); if (c.launched) throw new Error('launched should start as false');

explainGate.prompt: What is the difference between [disabled]="locked" and [disabled]="!locked" in an Angular template?

hints: [disabled]="expression" disables the button when the expression is truthy.
The button should be disabled when locked is true. So [disabled]="locked" is correct — not [disabled]="!locked".
Remove the ! from !locked in the disabled binding.

challenges.title: Two-Way Bridge
challenges.narrativeHook: One input. One property. They stay in sync. What does the banana-in-a-box syntax actually do?
challenges.instructions:
Read the component template below. `[(ngModel)]` binds the input's value to `callsign` in both directions — changes from the component update the input, and user input updates the component.

The user types `'Ryland'`. What is the value of `this.callsign` in the component class?
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// Answer: 'Ryland'. [(ngModel)] keeps the component property in sync with the input value.
challenges.predictPrompt: What is the value of this.callsign after the user types 'Ryland'?

explainGate.prompt: What two bindings does [(ngModel)] combine, and what is the equivalent longhand using [value] and (input)?

hints: [(ngModel)] is shorthand that combines property binding [ngModel] and event binding (ngModelChange).
The property binding flows data from the component to the input (input displays callsign). The event binding flows data back (user input updates callsign).
When the user types 'Ryland', the (ngModelChange) event fires and sets this.callsign = 'Ryland'.

challenges.title: Pipe the Data
challenges.narrativeHook: Raw numbers. Raw dates. Pipes transform them into something readable.
challenges.instructions:
Complete the `ReadoutComponent` class. The template uses three values:

- `temperature` — a number. The template shows it as `{{ temperature | number:'1.1-1' }}°C`
- `lastSync` — a `Date`. The template shows it as `{{ lastSync | date:'HH:mm' }}`
- `statusClass` — a string used in `[class]` binding. Return `'status-ok'` if `temperature < 80`, otherwise `'status-warn'`

The template is read-only. Implement the three class members.

files.name: readout.component.ts

tests.testCode: const c = new ReadoutComponent(); if (typeof c.temperature !== 'number') throw new Error(`temperature should be a number, got ${typeof c.temperature}`);
const c = new ReadoutComponent(); if (!(c.lastSync instanceof Date)) throw new Error('lastSync should be a Date object');
const c = new ReadoutComponent(); c.temperature = 72; if (c.statusClass !== 'status-ok') throw new Error(`Expected 'status-ok' for temp 72, got '${c.statusClass}'`);
const c = new ReadoutComponent(); c.temperature = 95; if (c.statusClass !== 'status-warn') throw new Error(`Expected 'status-warn' for temp 95, got '${c.statusClass}'`);

hints: Declare temperature as a number property with a starting value (e.g. temperature = 72;).
Declare lastSync as a Date: lastSync = new Date();
The statusClass getter: get statusClass() { return this.temperature < 80 ? 'status-ok' : 'status-warn'; }
